source_label: Bitcoin Optech — Topic: Inheritance and timelocks
excerpt: Timelocked recovery paths (CSV-based or absolute height) are a clean way to encode inheritance: funds remain spendable by the primary key indefinitely, but become spendable by a backup key after a sufficient period of dormancy. The design separates the trigger (inactivity) from the access (the backup key) without requiring a third-party custodian.
body:
Source excerpt:
Timelocked recovery paths (CSV-based or absolute height) are a clean way to encode inheritance: funds remain spendable by the primary key indefinitely, but become spendable by a backup key after a sufficient period of dormancy. The design separates the trigger (inactivity) from the access (the backup key) without requiring a third-party custodian.

---

Cited by leaf "Inheritance and recovery planning":
An inheritance plan has to solve two opposite problems at once: your heirs must be able to recover the coins after you're gone, and nobody — including your heirs — must be able to take them while you're alive. Most amateur plans solve the first by sacrificing the second (a sealed envelope with the seed in your house) or solve the second by sacrificing the first (no plan, coins lost on death) [1]. The mature designs use cryptographic and legal separation in combination so that no single party holds enough to act unilaterally.

The two main design patterns: collaborative-multisig with a co-signing service, where the inheritance is enabled by a service holding one key of a 2-of-3 that releases its key under documented conditions (death certificate, attestation, time delay); and sole-signer with a timelock, where the funds become spendable by a backup key only after the wallet has been inactive for some long period. Both patterns separate the trigger from the access: the heir does not have the key on day one, but does have a defined path to obtain it [2].

The legal-versus-cryptographic question is the one most people get wrong. A will that says "my Bitcoin goes to X" is enforceable in court but does not give X the keys; if no plan exists for X to actually obtain the keys, the will is useless. Conversely, handing your heir a working seed without legal documentation creates ambiguity and possibly tax exposure for them. The pattern that works in practice: a written letter (separate from the will) that explains the technical setup, references where each component lives, and doesn't itself contain any key material; combined with a wallet design that means following the letter is sufficient to recover the funds; combined with a legal will that names the heir and authorizes them to follow the letter [3]. None of those parts is enough alone.
